message.content:
Implement EXACTLY ONE task end-to-end in your isolated git worktree, run the gate, commit on the task branch, return the structured result JSON. Do NOT mutate the ledger; do NOT merge.

Task id: T106 (ledgerRefs goals:G6)
Branch: implement/T106    Base commit: c30ac49 (current main HEAD). If cut from a stale HEAD, `git reset --hard c30ac49` first; confirm config.BindMode / config.Path.Bind exist (T105 landed) before starting.
headline: Honor the bind mode in path-socket planning (I5 wiring)
description: Thread the resolved per-path bind mode into planPathBinds/selectDeviceBinds (internal/bind/pathsock.go): `source` forces source-IP pinning (never SO_BINDTODEVICE — the D38 topology's escape hatch), `device` forces device-bind for the path's interface (falling back to source with a WARN when the interface cannot be resolved or the setsockopt fails, matching the existing CAP fallback), `auto` keeps the current one-address-one-path heuristic byte-for-byte. Applies to Open, AddPath, and the deferred-path reconcile alike.
acceptance: Unit tests on selectDeviceBinds/planPathBinds cover all three modes including the forced-source case on a one-address interface (which auto would device-bind — the exact D38 trap) and the device-mode fallback path; auto mode reproduces the current planPathBinds output on the existing fixtures. Relates D38 without depending on its fix. go test ./... green.

NOTE: T105 (merged) added config.BindMode ("source"|"device"|"auto") on config.Config (global) + config.Path (per-path), already normalized (path>global>auto). This task CONSUMES that resolved value in the bind layer. Read internal/bind/pathsock.go (planPathBinds/selectDeviceBinds + the existing CAP fallback), and how config.Path flows into the bind path (the Path→bind seam). Thread the per-path resolved Bind mode through. `auto` MUST reproduce today's output byte-for-byte on existing fixtures (regression-guard that).

Gate (run via `nix develop -c sh -c '<cmd>'`): `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...`. gofmt your changes. Mutation-verify the forced-source-on-one-address-interface test (the D38 trap) discriminates.
If your worktree is not reachable for edits, `git switch -c t106-fix` off c30ac49 in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha. Return {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

52		// (today's selectDeviceBinds heuristic) in normalize(), so an existing config
53		// with no `bind` anywhere keeps exactly today's per-path bind behaviour.

61	//     (SO_BINDTODEVICE) only when provably equivalent to pinning source_addr,
62	//     source-IP-bind otherwise.

449		// edge sends this path's datagrams to. It is unset on the concentrator (which
450		// learns edge endpoints from traffic) and optional on the edge: when a single
451		// public concentrator IP fronts all uplinks (the common source-routed case)
452		// the peer's wireguard endpoint is reused for every path; when the paths reach
453		// the concentrator on distinct addresses, set dest_addr per path.
454		DestAddr netip.AddrPort `toml:"-"`
455		// DestAddrRaw is the TOML string form of DestAddr; parsed in normalize.
456		DestAddrRaw string `toml:"dest_addr"`
457		// LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec is the OPERATOR-DECLARED bottleneck bandwidth of this
458		// uplink in bits/s, parsed from LinkBandwidthRaw in normalize. It is used ONLY to
459		// size the weighted scheduler's per-path pace from the bandwidth-delay product
460		// (SizePacingFromBDP) when the weighted policy runs with pacing ENABLED (T53, Q20);
461		// it is OPERATOR-DECLARED, not runtime-measured — wanbond never auto-tunes it live.

463		LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec float64 `toml:"-"`
464		// LinkBandwidthRaw is the TOML string form of the declared bandwidth, e.g.

622	func (m *Multipath) Open(port uint16) ([]ReceiveFunc, uint16, error) {
623		m.mu.Lock()
624		defer m.mu.Unlock()
625		if len(m.paths) != 0 {
626			return nil, 0, conn.ErrBindAlreadyOpen
627		}
628	
629		// (Re)build the per-Open datapath planes — send Codec, receive resequencer, and FEC
630		// send/receive state — fresh for this bring-up, for EVERY bound peer (not just the
631		// primary via promotion). This keeps Open symmetric with closeSocketsLocked, which
632		// clears every peer's per-Open state: a concentrator peer bound before Open must get
633		// its OWN fresh planes on each Close→Open cycle, and one peer's (re)creation must never
634		// touch another peer's release point or FEC group state. On the single-peer edge/hub
635		// m.peers holds only the primary, so this is byte-identical to the pre-split rebuild.
636		// See openPeerDatapathLocked.
637		for _, p := range m.peers {
638			if err := m.openPeerDatapathLocked(p); err != nil {
639				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
640				return nil, 0, err
641			}
642		}
643	
644		// Resolve every path's interface up front and decide, per path, whether its
645		// socket may be device-bound (SO_BINDTODEVICE + wildcard — survives a T16
646		// re-roam) or must pin the specific source IP (the pre-T16 behaviour, required
647		// when paths share an interface or the interface is multi-address, so distinct
648		// specific-IP sockets coexist on one port without an EADDRINUSE collision). See
649		// selectDeviceBinds.
650		srcs := make([]netip.Addr, len(m.defs))
651		for i := range m.defs {
652			srcs[i] = m.defs[i].SourceAddr
653		}
654		bindDevs := planPathBinds(srcs)
655	
656		// A path whose WELL-FORMED source_addr is merely NOT-YET-ASSIGNABLE at boot
657		// (EADDRNOTAVAIL: no interface holds the address — a 5G modem without a DHCP
658		// lease, Starlink mid-obstruction) is DEFERRED rather than treated as fatal, so
659		// the bond comes up on the paths that DO bind (G2/W1, approach A). Tolerating it
660		// requires the runtime path-down machinery: the deferred path's prober stays
661		// StateDown so the scheduler excludes it, and a DynamicScheduler whose membership
662		// can be reconciled to only the bound paths. Absent the probe transport (the T12
663		// no-prober unit binds) or a dynamic scheduler there is no such Down model, so —
664		// exactly as AddPath refuses a runtime add without probers — every bind error
665		// stays fatal there. A MALFORMED source_addr never reaches here (config.validate
666		// rejects it at load), and any OTHER bind error (EADDRINUSE, permission) is fatal.
667		dyn, dynOK := m.scheduler.(sched.DynamicScheduler)
668		tolerateDefer := m.probers != nil && dynOK
669		m.deferred = nil
670	
671		actualPort := port
672		firstBound := true
673		// boundProbers is index-aligned with m.paths (the paths that actually bound), so
674		// the scheduler reconcile below and Send's Pick->m.paths[idx] mapping stay aligned
675		// even when a middle path deferred. nil in the no-prober case (nothing to reconcile).
676		var boundProbers []*telemetry.Prober
677		for i := range m.defs {
678			def := m.defs[i]
679			// Device-bind this path when selectDeviceBinds proved it safe (so a
680			// mid-session source-address change / T16 re-roam does not break the socket),
681			// otherwise pin the specific source IP. See selectDeviceBinds / listenPath.
682			c, err := listenPath(def.SourceAddr, port, bindDevs[i])
683			if err != nil {
684				if tolerateDefer && errors.Is(err, syscall.EADDRNOTAVAIL) {
685					// Defer this path: record its def + boot prober (kept Down) for the T55
686					// background reconcile to retry, and leave the bond to come up on the rest.
687					// Mirrors AddPath's rollback discipline — a failed path never disturbs the
688					// tunnel — at the boot boundary.
689					m.deferred = append(m.deferred, deferredPath{def: def, prober: m.probers[i]})
690					continue
691				}
692				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
693				return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: open path %q on %s: %w", def.Name, def.SourceAddr, err)
694			}
695			// Large SO_RCVBUF, best-effort: the kernel caps at net.core.rmem_max and
696			// SetReadBuffer does not require privilege, so a returned error is rare;
697			// treat it as non-fatal rather than refusing to bind in a restricted env.
698			_ = c.SetReadBuffer(socketRecvBuffer)
699	
700			// The path binds to the primary peer here; its receive codec is that peer's codec
701			// (reached through the embedded primary, as the datapath reaches m.sendCodec).
702			codec, err := m.newCodec()
703			if err != nil {
704				_ = c.Close()
705				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
706				return nil, 0, err
707			}
708	
709			shared := &sharedPathState{name: def.Name, id: uint8(i), src: def.SourceAddr, conn: c}
710			ps := &peerPathState{sharedPathState: shared, peer: m.peerState, codec: codec}
711			if m.probers != nil {
712				ps.prober = m.probers[i]
713				// Reconcile the DATA-frame path-id to the prober's IMMUTABLE stamp rather than
714				// to the slice index i: after a runtime RemovePath the survivor keeps its
715				// original (higher) stamp, so index-based numbering would renumber a live path
716				// AND diverge its DATA id from its PROBE stamp. Taking id from the prober keeps
717				// DATA and PROBE agreeing on the wire and a survivor's id stable across a reopen.
718				shared.id = ps.prober.PathID()
719				boundProbers = append(boundProbers, ps.prober)
720			}
721			switch {
722			case def.DestAddr.IsValid():
723				ps.setRemote(def.DestAddr)
724			case m.hasDefaultRemote:
725				ps.setRemote(m.defaultRemote)
726			}
727			m.shared = append(m.shared, shared)
728			m.paths = append(m.paths, ps)
729			// Publish the primary's view for the receive demux (T88). On the single-peer edge/hub
730			// this is the ONLY view, which handleInbound reads as the byte-identical fast path; a
731			// concentrator peer bound later appends its own view to the same socket.
732			shared.addViewLocked(ps)
733			if firstBound {
734				actualPort = uint16(c.LocalAddr().(*net.UDPAddr).Port)
735				firstBound = false
736			}
737		}
738	
739		// Hard guard (a): a bond with NO transport is impossible. If every configured path
740		// deferred, tolerance must NOT degrade to a zero-path bind — fail fatally, exactly
741		// as the pre-tolerance Open did when the sole path could not bind.
742		if len(m.paths) == 0 {
743			_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
744			return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: no configured path could bind its source address (all %d deferred as not-yet-assignable)", len(m.deferred))
745		}
746	
747		// nextPathID is a MONOTONIC HIGH-WATER carried ACROSS Open spans, never decreased.
748		// Close does not reset it, and here it is only ever RAISED to at least max(existing
749		// path stamp)+1. Resetting it to len(m.paths) (the pre-fix behaviour) re-minted an id
750		// a survivor still held once a RemovePath had opened a gap in the stamp space below
751		// that survivor's stamp: the next runtime AddPath then collided with the live path at
752		// an identical (PathID, SessionID), and because the peer's Reflector keys anti-replay
753		// AND the session challenge PER PathID, the strict-monotonic replay filter dropped one
754		// of the two independent ProbeSeq streams -> probe loss / false-DOWN. Deriving the
755		// high-water from the (prober-stamped) ps.id covers both the probe-transport case and
756		// the T12 no-prober case (where ps.id == i, so the high-water is len(m.paths)).
757		m.openPort = port
758		for _, ps := range m.paths {
759			if uint16(ps.id)+1 > m.nextPathID {
760				m.nextPathID = uint16(ps.id) + 1
761			}
762		}
763		// A DEFERRED path's boot prober already OWNS its path-id stamp; raise the
764		// high-water past it too, so a runtime AddPath arriving while the path is still
765		// deferred cannot mint that reserved id and collide with it once T55 binds it.
766		for _, dp := range m.deferred {
767			if dp.prober != nil {
768				if uint16(dp.prober.PathID())+1 > m.nextPathID {
769					m.nextPathID = uint16(dp.prober.PathID()) + 1
770				}
771			}
772		}
773	
774		// Reconcile the scheduler's membership with the path slice just rebuilt from
775		// m.defs. A runtime AddPath/RemovePath (T30) keeps m.defs, m.probers, AND the
776		// live scheduler in lockstep during an Open span; re-pinning the scheduler's
777		// health list to the BOUND probers HERE — index-aligned with m.paths, since each
778		// bound path appended its ps.prober to boundProbers in order above — is the single
779		// reconciliation point that makes that runtime membership survive this
780		// Close→Open cycle without a scheduler/path desync (no frozen zombie health
781		// entry, no resurrected removed path). A DEFERRED path (not-yet-assignable

1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"net"
6		"net/netip"
7		"sync"
8		"time"
9	
10		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/sched"
11	)
12	
13	// DefaultReconcileInterval is the cadence at which StartReconcileLoop re-attempts the
14	// bind of the paths a tolerant Open (T51) left DEFERRED. It is a seconds-scale poll,
15	// not the sub-second probe cadence: a deferred path is a boot-time transient (a 5G
16	// modem acquiring a DHCP lease, Starlink emerging from an obstruction), an event that
17	// resolves on a human/network timescale, so a 1s cadence bounds the promote latency
18	// tightly while the tick — a single mutex-guarded length check when nothing is
19	// deferred (the steady state) — stays negligible.
20	const DefaultReconcileInterval = 1 * time.Second
21	
22	// defaultDeferredListen is the production bind for a reconciled deferred path: it pins
23	// the path's source IP on the bind's shared port, identical to AddPath's runtime bind
24	// (net.ListenUDP on the source). It returns EADDRNOTAVAIL for as long as no interface
25	// holds the address — which is exactly the signal reconcileDeferred reads to keep the
26	// path deferred and retry — and succeeds once the address becomes assignable.
27	func defaultDeferredListen(src netip.Addr, port uint16) (*net.UDPConn, error) {
28		laddr := &net.UDPAddr{IP: net.IP(src.AsSlice()), Port: int(port)}
29		return net.ListenUDP("udp", laddr)
30	}
31	
32	// StartReconcileLoop launches the background deferred-path reconciler (T55): every
33	// interval it re-attempts the bind of the paths a tolerant Open (T51) left DEFERRED
34	// (a well-formed source_addr that was not yet assignable at boot) and PROMOTES any
35	// that now binds to a live path — into m.paths, the scheduler, and its own reader —
36	// so a source_addr that becomes assignable after boot (a 5G modem that finally got
37	// its DHCP lease) is brought into the running bond WITHOUT a restart. It stops when
38	// the returned stopper is invoked (idempotent), so the daemon shuts it down before
39	// Close with no goroutine leak — the symmetric companion to StartProbeLoop. It is a
40	// no-op (returning a no-op stopper) on a bind without the probe transport (which never
41	// defers — Open makes every bind error fatal there) or interval <= 0.
42	//
43	// Mechanism (design decision): a bounded periodic POLL, not netlink route/addr
44	// subscription. Event-driven netlink (vishvananda/netlink AddrSubscribe) would add a
45	// new external dependency this module does not carry, and the deferred set is normally
46	// EMPTY (every path bound at boot), so the poll degenerates to a cheap length check;
47	// when a path is deferred, re-attempting a non-blocking ListenUDP once per interval
48	// promotes it within a bounded window. A fixed interval rather than a backoff: the
49	// deferred window is a bounded boot-time transient, so a steady cadence bounds the
50	// promote latency without a backoff schedule's complexity.
51	//
52	// The loop owns its own done channel (like StartProbeLoop) rather than riding the
53	// per-Open recvClosed: it is a DEVICE-lifecycle goroutine started after the first Open
54	// and stopped before Close, and it operates on whatever m.deferred the current Open
55	// span holds (a Close→Open rebuilds m.deferred; reconcileDeferred is a no-op while the
56	// bind is closed). Its cadence uses a wall-clock ticker because it is production timing
57	// glue; the bind mutation it performs is exercised directly in tests against the
58	// injected listen seam, so a test never starts this goroutine.
59	func (m *Multipath) StartReconcileLoop(interval time.Duration) (stop func()) {
60		if m.newProber == nil || interval <= 0 {
61			return func() {}
62		}
63		done := make(chan struct{})
64		go func() {
65			ticker := time.NewTicker(interval)
66			defer ticker.Stop()
67			for {
68				select {
69				case <-done:
70					return
71				case <-ticker.C:
72					m.reconcileDeferred()
73				}
74			}
75		}()
76		var once sync.Once
77		return func() { once.Do(func() { close(done) }) }
78	}
79	
80	// reconcileDeferred is one background reconcile step (T55): it re-attempts the bind of
81	// every DEFERRED path and promotes any that now binds to a live path, leaving the rest
82	// deferred for the next tick. A path that still cannot bind — EADDRNOTAVAIL (address
83	// still not assignable) OR any other transient bind error — stays deferred and is
84	// retried; a bind fault never becomes fatal to the RUNNING bond (the tunnel is already
85	// up on the paths that bound). It runs entirely under m.mu, so it serializes with
86	// Send/Close/AddPath/RemovePath and the path slice + scheduler mutate together, and it
87	// is a no-op on a CLOSED bind (len(m.paths)==0) — so it races a concurrent Close
88	// harmlessly (Close either ran first, and this no-ops, or runs after and joins the
89	// reader this step just spawned via readersWG) — or when nothing is deferred (the
90	// steady state, a single length check).
91	func (m *Multipath) reconcileDeferred() {
92		m.mu.Lock()
93		defer m.mu.Unlock()
94		if len(m.paths) == 0 || len(m.deferred) == 0 {
95			return // closed, or nothing to promote
96		}
97		// A deferred path exists only under the runtime path-down model tolerance requires
98		// (probe transport + DynamicScheduler); Open never defers without it, so this
99		// assertion holds. Guard defensively rather than type-assert-panic on the hot lock.
100		dyn, ok := m.scheduler.(sched.DynamicScheduler)
101		if !ok {
102			return
103		}
104		// In-place filter: keep the paths that did NOT promote this tick. kept aliases the
105		// deferred backing array, and its length never exceeds the loop index, so appending
106		// a still-deferred entry never clobbers one not yet read (the standard filter idiom).
107		kept := m.deferred[:0]
108		for _, dp := range m.deferred {
109			c, err := m.deferredListen(dp.def.SourceAddr, m.openPort)
110			if err != nil {
111				kept = append(kept, dp) // still not assignable (or a transient fault): retry next tick
112				continue
113			}
114			if err := m.promoteDeferredLocked(dyn, dp, c); err != nil {
115				// The bind succeeded but promotion did not (a scheduler/path index skew, or a
116				// codec build error): close the fresh socket and keep the path deferred so the
117				// next tick retries cleanly, rather than leaking the socket or half-admitting.
118				_ = c.Close()
119				kept = append(kept, dp)
120			}
121		}
122		m.deferred = kept
123	}
124	
125	// promoteDeferredLocked turns a just-bound deferred path into a LIVE one under m.mu. It
126	// wraps the fresh socket in a pathState that REUSES the deferred path's preserved boot
127	// prober — so the promoted path keeps its reserved path-id STAMP (no renumber, no
128	// collision with the peer's per-path reflector state), the continuity T51 preserved for
129	// exactly this step — seeds its remote from the config dest_addr or the learned default,
130	// appends it to m.paths, admits its prober to the scheduler as the new lowest-priority
131	// path, and spawns its Bind-owned reader. It mirrors AddPath's admission MINUS minting a
132	// prober and MINUS touching the durable membership (a deferred path is ALREADY in
133	// m.defs/m.probers and its id-stamp is ALREADY reserved in m.nextPathID by Open), so the
134	// caller need only drop it from m.deferred on success. It rolls a failed admission back
135	// completely and returns the error, leaving m.paths and the scheduler as it found them.
136	func (m *Multipath) promoteDeferredLocked(dyn sched.DynamicScheduler, dp deferredPath, c *net.UDPConn) error {
137		// Large SO_RCVBUF, best-effort (kernel-capped, needs no privilege) — as in Open/AddPath.
138		_ = c.SetReadBuffer(socketRecvBuffer)
139		// The deferred-path machinery is single-peer today, so this attaches the primary peer's
140		// view; its receive codec is that peer's codec, reached through the embedded primary.
141		codec, err := m.newCodec()
142		if err != nil {
143			return err // programmer error (psk validated at construction); never hit in practice
144		}
145		// Reuse the boot prober's IMMUTABLE stamp for the DATA-frame path-id, exactly as Open
146		// and AddPath do, so DATA and PROBE agree on the wire and the promoted path is never
147		// renumbered. The deferred-path machinery is single-peer today (the concentrator fan-out
148		// of a promoted deferred path is a later G4 task), so this attaches the primary peer's
149		// view over the freshly-bound shared socket.
150		shared := &sharedPathState{name: dp.def.Name, id: dp.prober.PathID(), src: dp.def.SourceAddr, conn: c}
151		ps := &peerPathState{sharedPathState: shared, peer: m.peerState, codec: codec, prober: dp.prober}
152		switch {
153		case dp.def.DestAddr.IsValid():
154			ps.setRemote(dp.def.DestAddr)
155		case m.hasDefaultRemote:
156			ps.setRemote(m.defaultRemote)
157		}
158		// Append to the shared socket list + the peer's path slice, then admit the prober to the
159		// scheduler as the new tail; the peer's path slice and its scheduler are index-aligned,
160		// so the scheduler's returned index must equal the new path's slice index. A mismatch
161		// would mis-route datagrams, so fail loudly and roll back.
162		m.shared = append(m.shared, shared)
163		m.paths = append(m.paths, ps)
164		// Publish the primary's view of the freshly-bound socket for the receive demux (T88);
165		// single-peer today (the deferred-path concentrator fan-out is a later G4 task), so this
166		// is the socket's only view and handleInbound reads it as the fast path.
167		shared.addViewLocked(ps)
168		schedIdx, err := dyn.AddPath(ps.prober)
169		if err != nil {
170			m.paths = m.paths[:len(m.paths)-1]
171			m.shared = m.shared[:len(m.shared)-1]
172			return err
173		}
174		if schedIdx != len(m.paths)-1 {
175			bindIdx := len(m.paths) - 1
176			_ = dyn.RemovePath(schedIdx)
177			m.paths = m.paths[:len(m.paths)-1]
178			m.shared = m.shared[:len(m.shared)-1]
179			return fmt.Errorf("bind: scheduler/path index skew after deferred promote: sched=%d bind=%d", schedIdx, bindIdx)
180		}
181		// Spawn the promoted path's Bind-owned reader on the CURRENT Open span's delivery
182		// signal; readersWG tracks it so a subsequent Close joins it (no goroutine leak).
183		m.readersWG.Add(1)
184		go m.readLoop(ps, m.deliverSignal)
185		return nil
186	}

1	package log
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"io"
6		"log/slog"
7		"strings"
8	)
9	
10	// Field key conventions. Per-path events carry FieldPath so logs can be filtered
11	// by uplink; every component-scoped logger carries FieldComponent.
12	const (
13		FieldComponent = "component"
14		FieldPath      = "path"
15	)
16	
17	// Logger is the structured-logging surface the rest of wanbond depends on, so no
18	// package imports log/slog directly. It supports component- and path-scoped
19	// child loggers whose scope attributes are attached to every subsequent record.
20	type Logger interface {
21		Debug(msg string, args ...any)
22		Info(msg string, args ...any)
23		Warn(msg string, args ...any)
24		Error(msg string, args ...any)
25		// Component returns a child logger tagged with the given component name.
26		Component(name string) Logger
27		// Path returns a child logger tagged with the given path name.
28		Path(name string) Logger
29	}
30	
31	// slogLogger is the slog-backed implementation of Logger.
32	type slogLogger struct {
33		l *slog.Logger
34	}
35	
36	// New builds a JSON-handler Logger writing to w at the given level. An unknown
37	// level is a configuration error (fail fast); an empty level defaults to info.
38	func New(level string, w io.Writer) (Logger, error) {
39		lvl, err := parseLevel(level)
40		if err != nil {
41			return nil, err
42		}
43		h := slog.NewJSONHandler(w, &slog.HandlerOptions{Level: lvl})
44		return &slogLogger{l: slog.New(h)}, nil
45	}
46	
47	func parseLevel(level string) (slog.Level, error) {
48		switch strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(level)) {
49		case "", "info":
50			return slog.LevelInfo, nil
51		case "debug":
52			return slog.LevelDebug, nil
53		case "warn", "warning":
54			return slog.LevelWarn, nil
55		case "error":
56			return slog.LevelError, nil
57		default:
58			return 0, fmt.Errorf("log: unknown level %q (want debug|info|warn|error)", level)
59		}
60	}
61	
62	func (s *slogLogger) Debug(msg string, args ...any) { s.l.Debug(msg, args...) }
63	func (s *slogLogger) Info(msg string, args ...any)  { s.l.Info(msg, args...) }
64	func (s *slogLogger) Warn(msg string, args ...any)  { s.l.Warn(msg, args...) }
65	func (s *slogLogger) Error(msg string, args ...any) { s.l.Error(msg, args...) }
66	
67	func (s *slogLogger) Component(name string) Logger {
68		return &slogLogger{l: s.l.With(FieldComponent, name)}
69	}
70	
71	func (s *slogLogger) Path(name string) Logger {
72		return &slogLogger{l: s.l.With(FieldPath, name)}
73	}

- description: "[fixes-doc D3, S2 — production deploy.] On every start the edge logs one `Failed to send handshake initiation: bind: no healthy path with a known remote endpoint` (paths not up yet); paths transition Up ~0.6 s later — and then nothing: the first init is wasted and re-initiation is not visibly driven off the path-up edge, leaving the tunnel waiting on WG's own retransmit timers. Compounds the one-sided-restart outage (defects:D36). WG should (re)initiate the moment the first path becomes live. Related observability: the same startup window logs `no healthy path` at ERROR during the normal ~1 s liveness warmup (fixes-doc I4 — downgrade/rate-limit it; tracked in the wanbond-fixes goal)."
- suggestedFix: "Drive a handshake (re)initiation off the first path StateUp transition (liveness edge → poke the engine to SendHandshakeInitiation / expire-and-retry), instead of relying on the engine's own back-off after a failed send. Validate in the netns e2e: from cold start, time-to-first-handshake should track path-up (+one RTT), not a retransmit timer."

- author: fable-5

- headline: Auto device-bind silently defeats `ip rule from <source_addr>` policy routing on single-address VLAN-per-WAN edges
- severity: high
- description: "[fixes-doc D4, S1 — production deploy; workaround applied on the Pi.] selectDeviceBinds (internal/bind/pathsock.go) picks SO_BINDTODEVICE (wildcard source) for a one-address/one-path interface — EXACTLY the VLAN-per-WAN edge topology (eth0.231/eth0.232, one address each, per-WAN pinning via `ip rule from <source_addr>`). A wildcard-source socket never matches `ip rule from <source_addr>`, so the route lookup falls through to `main` (which has no route to the concentrator via that VLAN) → ENETUNREACH → silent path-down, ZERO packets on the wire, while `ping -I <source_ip>` proves the WAN works. Worked around in production with `ip rule add oif <dev> table N`. The T16/R23 conditional device-bind gating (single-address + single-path interface) was designed for roam survival but is precisely the wrong choice under source-based policy routing — the two selection criteria collide. Related: defects:D30 (runtime-added paths never device-bind — the mirror-image gap)."
- suggestedFix: "Two parts: (1) a config toggle to force source-IP binding per path or globally (`bind = \"source\"`) so source-policy-routing topologies can opt out of device-bind (fixes-doc I5; roam survival is moot there); consider making that mode the DEFAULT when the config implies source-routed WANs, or detecting the `from <source>` rule. (2) Document the collision + the `ip rule add oif <dev> table N` recipe until then (fixes-doc C2). Decide in plan-flow whether detection is feasible or the toggle suffices."

- createdAt: 2026-07-13T22:49:13.912Z
- updatedAt: 2026-07-13T22:49:13.912Z
- author: fable-5
- session: cac93b81-5292-42e3-b77e-962544c75e54
- headline: "wanbond0 left DOWN + unaddressed; NetworkManager flushes the operator's address → cryptic `TUN write: input/output error`"
- severity: medium
- description: "[fixes-doc D5, S2 — production deploy; workaround applied on the Pi.] The daemon creates the tun but never brings the link UP or addresses it. On a NetworkManager host (RPi OS / Debian / Ubuntu desktop — the common edge) the tun is auto-managed: NM FLUSHES the operator's address on link-up and the interface stays DOWN across daemon restarts. Writing into it then yields the cryptic `TUN write: input/output error`. Worked around with NM `unmanaged-devices=interface-name:wanbond0` + an addressing oneshot unit. Note the shipped docs (T22/R27, docs/install.md §4) cover only systemd-networkd addressing — the NM case is undocumented (fixes-doc C1). Fix direction split across: daemon brings the link UP itself (fixes-doc I1, low-risk; addressing stays operator-owned), actionable EIO diagnostics (fixes-doc I3), interface/route persistence across restarts (fixes-doc I7), and the NM + oneshot doc recipes (fixes-doc C1/C4) — the improvements/doc side is tracked in the wanbond-fixes goal; this defect item covers the DOWN-link/NM failure mode itself."
- suggestedFix: "Minimum code fix: bring wanbond0 UP after creating it (link-up only; addressing remains operator-owned), and on TUN write EIO check link state/MTU and emit an actionable error (e.g. 'wanbond0 is DOWN — address & bring it up (install.md §4)'). Document NM unmanaged-devices + the PartOf oneshot pattern. Consider keeping the tun persistent across daemon restarts so addresses/routes/rules referencing it survive (I7)."
- sourceRefs: ["wanbond-fixes.md §A D5","wanbond-fixes.md §B I1","wanbond-fixes.md §B I3","wanbond-fixes.md §B I7","wanbond-fixes.md §C C1","wanbond-fixes.md §C C4"]

- headline: CAP_NET_RAW (pathsock comment) vs CAP_NET_ADMIN (shipped systemd units) mismatch for SO_BINDTODEVICE
- severity: low

1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"net/netip"
5		"testing"
6	)
7	
8	// TestSelectDeviceBinds is the T16 rework regression for the device-bind SELECTION
9	// rule (the two review criticisms fable reproduced under `unshare -Urn`). Device
10	// binding (SO_BINDTODEVICE + wildcard source — the roam-surviving mode) must be
11	// chosen ONLY when it is provably equivalent to pinning the configured
12	// source_addr AND no other path contends for the device; every other path must
13	// fall back to source-IP binding (the pre-T16 behaviour that lets distinct
14	// specific-IP sockets coexist on one port). It exercises selectDeviceBinds with a
15	// fake resolver so it needs no privilege / real interfaces.
16	func TestSelectDeviceBinds(t *testing.T) {
17		addr := func(s string) netip.Addr { return netip.MustParseAddr(s) }
18	
19		// build wraps a source-address -> ifaceInfo table as a resolver.
20		build := func(table map[netip.Addr]ifaceInfo) func(netip.Addr) ifaceInfo {
21			return func(a netip.Addr) ifaceInfo { return table[a] }
22		}
23	
24		tests := []struct {
25			name    string
26			srcs    []netip.Addr
27			table   map[netip.Addr]ifaceInfo
28			wantDev []string
29		}{
30			{
31				// A solo path on a single-address interface: device-bind (roam benefit).
32				name:    "solo single-address interface device-binds",
33				srcs:    []netip.Addr{addr("198.51.100.10")},
34				table:   map[netip.Addr]ifaceInfo{addr("198.51.100.10"): {dev: "eth0", familyCount: 1}},
35				wantDev: []string{"eth0"},
36			},
37			{
38				// Criticism 1: two paths whose sources live on the SAME interface. Neither
39				// may device-bind (the second wildcard+device socket collides EADDRINUSE);
40				// both source-IP-bind so their two distinct specific-IP sockets coexist.
41				name: "shared interface -> all source-IP-bound",
42				srcs: []netip.Addr{addr("192.0.2.10"), addr("192.0.2.11")},
43				table: map[netip.Addr]ifaceInfo{
44					addr("192.0.2.10"): {dev: "eth0", familyCount: 2},
45					addr("192.0.2.11"): {dev: "eth0", familyCount: 2},
46				},
47				wantDev: []string{"", ""},
48			},
49			{
50				// Criticism 2: a solo path on a MULTI-address interface. A wildcard+device
51				// socket would let the kernel pick a different source, voiding the pin, so
52				// it must source-IP-bind despite being the only path on the device.
53				name:    "solo multi-address interface -> source-IP-bound",
54				srcs:    []netip.Addr{addr("203.0.113.5")},
55				table:   map[netip.Addr]ifaceInfo{addr("203.0.113.5"): {dev: "eth0", familyCount: 2}},
56				wantDev: []string{""},
57			},
58			{
59				// A source that resolves to no interface (loopback / unresolved) never
60				// device-binds — it falls straight through to source-IP binding.
61				name:    "unresolved source -> source-IP-bound",
62				srcs:    []netip.Addr{addr("127.0.0.1")},
63				table:   map[netip.Addr]ifaceInfo{},
64				wantDev: []string{""},
65			},
66			{
67				// The mixed real case: two solo single-address uplinks on distinct devices
68				// both device-bind, while a third pair sharing a device does not — the
69				// decision is per-path, not all-or-nothing.
70				name: "two solo uplinks device-bind, shared pair does not",
71				srcs: []netip.Addr{addr("198.51.100.10"), addr("203.0.113.20"), addr("192.0.2.10"), addr("192.0.2.11")},
72				table: map[netip.Addr]ifaceInfo{
73					addr("198.51.100.10"): {dev: "wan0", familyCount: 1},
74					addr("203.0.113.20"):  {dev: "wan1", familyCount: 1},
75					addr("192.0.2.10"):    {dev: "lan0", familyCount: 2},
76					addr("192.0.2.11"):    {dev: "lan0", familyCount: 2},
77				},
78				wantDev: []string{"wan0", "wan1", "", ""},
79			},
80		}
81	
82		for _, tc := range tests {
83			t.Run(tc.name, func(t *testing.T) {
84				got := selectDeviceBinds(tc.srcs, build(tc.table))
85				if len(got) != len(tc.wantDev) {
86					t.Fatalf("selectDeviceBinds returned %d entries, want %d (%v)", len(got), len(tc.wantDev), got)
87				}
88				for i := range got {
89					if got[i] != tc.wantDev[i] {
90						t.Fatalf("path %d bind = %q, want %q (full: %v want %v)", i, got[i], tc.wantDev[i], got, tc.wantDev)
91					}
92				}
93			})
94		}
95	}
96	
97	// TestFamilyBindCount is the D13 regression for interfaceInfo's family-count rule.
98	// An up interface virtually always carries a kernel fe80::/10 link-local alongside
99	// its configured global v6 address; counting the link-local pushed familyCount>=2
100	// so a GLOBAL v6 source never qualified for device binding (T16 re-roam survival).
101	// The kernel never source-selects a link-local for a global destination, so the
102	// link-local must be EXCLUDED from the count of a global v6 source — while a v4
103	// source, and a link-local v6 source, count every same-family address unchanged.
104	func TestFamilyBindCount(t *testing.T) {
105		addr := func(s string) netip.Addr { return netip.MustParseAddr(s) }
106		globalV6 := addr("2001:db8::10")
107		linkLocalV6 := addr("fe80::1")
108		otherGlobalV6 := addr("2001:db8::11")
109	
110		tests := []struct {
111			name       string
112			want       netip.Addr
113			addrs      []netip.Addr
114			wantFamily int
115			wantOwns   bool
116		}{
117			{
118				// The defect case: a global v6 source alongside the kernel link-local.
119				// The link-local must not count, so familyCount == 1 and the source
120				// still qualifies for device binding.
121				name:       "global v6 with kernel link-local excludes link-local",
122				want:       globalV6,
123				addrs:      []netip.Addr{globalV6, linkLocalV6},
124				wantFamily: 1,
125				wantOwns:   true,
126			},
127			{
128				// Two GLOBAL v6 addresses genuinely contend: both count, so the source
129				// does not qualify (a wildcard+device socket could source from either).
130				name:       "two global v6 addresses both count",
131				want:       globalV6,
132				addrs:      []netip.Addr{globalV6, otherGlobalV6, linkLocalV6},
133				wantFamily: 2,
134				wantOwns:   true,
135			},
136			{
137				// A link-local v6 SOURCE is unaffected: every same-family (incl.
138				// link-local) address counts, exactly as before.
139				name:       "link-local v6 source counts link-locals",
140				want:       linkLocalV6,
141				addrs:      []netip.Addr{linkLocalV6, addr("fe80::2")},
142				wantFamily: 2,
143				wantOwns:   true,
144			},
145			{
146				// A v4 source is unaffected by the v6 rule; a coexisting v6 (of either
147				// scope) is a different family and never counts.
148				name:       "v4 source unaffected by v6 addresses",
149				want:       addr("192.0.2.10"),
150				addrs:      []netip.Addr{addr("192.0.2.10"), globalV6, linkLocalV6},
151				wantFamily: 1,
152				wantOwns:   true,
153			},
154		}
155	
156		for _, tc := range tests {
157			t.Run(tc.name, func(t *testing.T) {
158				familyCount, owns := familyBindCount(tc.want, tc.addrs)
159				if familyCount != tc.wantFamily {

feat(bind): consume the resolved per-path bind mode in planPathBinds/selectDeviceBinds (T106, I5)

selectDeviceBinds/planPathBinds now take each path's resolved config.BindMode
(config.Path.Bind, T105) alongside its source address:

- BindModeSource forces source-IP pinning unconditionally, never
  SO_BINDTODEVICE — the D38 escape hatch for source-policy-routed
  (VLAN-per-WAN) uplinks the auto heuristic would otherwise device-bind,
  silently defeating `ip rule from <source_addr>`.
- BindModeDevice forces a device bind whenever the source's interface
  resolves, regardless of family count or contention with another path;
  it falls back to source-IP binding when the interface cannot be
  resolved, and (via listenPath's existing CAP fallback) when
  SO_BINDTODEVICE itself fails.
- BindModeAuto reproduces the pre-I5 one-address-one-path heuristic
  byte-for-byte (regression-guarded by re-running the existing fixtures
  under explicit BindModeAuto).

Wired into Open (via planPathBinds), and into AddPath and the T55
deferred-path reconcile (via a new resolveForcedDeviceBind helper +
threading dev through listenPath / the deferredListen seam) so a forced
BindModeDevice is honored consistently at runtime-add and promote time
too; BindModeSource/BindModeAuto keep AddPath/reconcile's pre-existing
source-IP-pin behaviour (D30, runtime auto device-bind, stays open).

Relates D38 without depending on its fix.
EOF
)"

### Session summary
- **Did:** implemented task T106 in worktree `implement/T106` (base reset to c30ac49, where T105 landed): threaded `config.BindMode` through `internal/bind/pathsock.go`'s `selectDeviceBinds`/`planPathBinds`, and wired the resolved per-path mode into `Open`, `AddPath`, and the T55 deferred-path reconcile.
- **Achieved:** pass; `resultCommit` a62595368c46ca7f930ebeeebad94970e72087e4; `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./... && gofmt -l .` all green.
- **Discovered:** `AddPath`/`reconcileDeferred` never called `planPathBinds`/`listenPath` before this task — they always `net.ListenUDP`'d directly (source-pin only), which is defect D30 ("runtime-added paths never device-bind"). I honored a forced `BindModeDevice` there too (new capability, via a new `resolveForcedDeviceBind` helper + threading `dev` through `listenPath`/the `deferredListen` seam), but deliberately left `BindModeSource`/`BindModeAuto` behavior at those two call sites unchanged (still always source-pin) since D30 itself stays open and out of this task's scope — only the forced-device case needed activating there per the task description ("Applies to Open, AddPath, and the deferred-path reconcile alike").
- **Issues:** The task description mentions emitting a WARN log on the device-mode fallback path. `internal/bind` carries no logger reference anywhere today (`Multipath` has no logger field; only `telemetry.Prober` internally holds one, unexported). Adding logging plumbing would mean changing `NewMultipath`'s constructor signature and every call site across cmd/tests — a materially larger, unscoped change not required by the acceptance criteria (which tests `selectDeviceBinds`/`planPathBinds` behavior only, not logging). I implemented the functional fallback behavior (falls back to source-IP binding silently, matching the existing silent CAP fallback in `listenPath`) but did not add new logging infrastructure. Flagging for the orchestrator/reviewer to decide whether a follow-up task for the WARN observability is warranted.

Files touched (all in `/home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a45dbb6e85fe5dcff/internal/bind/`):
- `multipath.go` — `Open` builds a `modes []config.BindMode` alongside `srcs`; `AddPath` now computes `dev` via `resolveForcedDeviceBind` and calls `listenPath` instead of raw `net.ListenUDP`; `deferredListen` field signature gained a `dev string` param.
- `pathsock.go` — `selectDeviceBinds`/`planPathBinds` take `modes []config.BindMode`; new `resolveForcedDeviceBind` helper for the single-path AddPath/reconcile case.
- `reconcile.go` — `defaultDeferredListen` now delegates to `listenPath(src, port, dev)`; `reconcileDeferred` computes `dev` per deferred path before calling the seam.
- `pathsock_select_test.go` — existing `TestSelectDeviceBinds` now runs under explicit `BindModeAuto` (byte-for-byte regression guard); added `TestSelectDeviceBindsForcedSource` (the D38-trap discriminator, mutation-verified to fail if the source case regresses to auto behavior), `TestSelectDeviceBindsForcedDevice` (resolvable-multi-address forced-bind + unresolvable-interface fallback), and `TestPlanPathBinds` (wrapper smoke test).
- `reconcile_test.go` — `fakeDeferredBinder.listen` signature updated for the new `dev` parameter.
